Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from the MADDRESS table, which can retrieve unnecessary data and hurt performance. Explicitly list only the required columns in the select clause.
Using `returning()` without specifying columns returns all columns from the MEMBER table, which may include unnecessary data. Specify only the columns needed, similar to the MAUTH update on line 607.

The LIKE condition is used for text searching where the pattern may contain wildcards anywhere in the string (as determined by user input containing '*' or '%' characters). This can lead to poor performance on large datasets since it cannot use standard indexes. Consider using a full-text search index or a dedicated search engine like Elasticsearch for better performance.

Using likeIgnoreCase for pattern matching can be inefficient for full-text search scenarios. Since the pattern 'eb%' is a prefix search (wildcard only at the end), this is actually acceptable for prefix matching, but if the intent is to search within usernames, consider using a full-text search index or a dedicated search engine.

This query only selects MEMBER.MID, which is a specific column projection, not all columns. However, looking more carefully, this is actually selecting only one specific column (MEMBER.MID), so this is NOT an implicit columns issue.
